content.text:
Here's what was added:

**`Weapon.cs`** â€” `showDebugArea` bool in a new Debug header. Toggling this on a MeleeWeapon will show its cone, and toggling it on a RangedWeapon/ThrowWeapon will do nothing on its own (the projectiles have their own toggle).

**`Projectile.cs`** â€” `showDebugArea` bool + `OnDrawGizmos`. When enabled on a projectile prefab that has `explodeOnDestroy`, it draws a semi-transparent orange sphere showing the explosion radius.

**`PlayerController.cs`** â€” `OnDrawGizmos` + `DrawMeleeDebugArea`. When the equipped melee weapon has `showDebugArea` on, it draws:
- A faint red sphere showing the full overlap sphere range
- Yellow lines for the cone edges (left, center, right)
- A yellow arc connecting the cone edges at max range

Make sure **Gizmos** are enabled in the Game view toolbar (the small Gizmos toggle button in the top-right of the Game view) to see them during play mode.

The projectile gizmo already only shows while the projectile is alive, so that's naturally attack-triggered. The melee cone just needs to be gated on the current state being the melee attack state.

content.text: Done. The melee cone now only draws while the player is in `PlayerMeleeAttackState` (for the duration of `attackDuration`). The projectile explosion gizmo was already naturally scoped to attack time since it only exists on live projectile GameObjects.

message.content: I would like that if explde on destroy is enable on a projectile, the area of effect be shown when it actually explodes

content.text: The projectile gets destroyed immediately after `Explode()` is called, so the gizmo never has a chance to render at the explosion point. I'll spawn a short-lived visual at the explosion position instead.
I'll remove the gizmo from Projectile and instead spawn a lightweight temporary GameObject with its own gizmo drawer at the explosion point.

content.text: Done. When a projectile with `showDebugArea` and `explodeOnDestroy` enabled explodes, it now spawns a temporary `ExplosionDebugGizmo` at the impact point that displays the explosion radius sphere for 1 second before cleaning itself up. The gizmo is no longer drawn while the projectile is in flight.

message.content: I don't know if that's the case, but I want you to adjust the melee area of effect to be affected by the attack height offset of the weapon

content.text: Let me check the current melee debug drawing code.

It's already using `attackHeightOffset` â€” line 328 sets the origin to `this.transform.position + Vector3.up * this.meleeWeapon.attackHeightOffset`, which matches exactly how the actual `MeleeWeapon.Use()` calculates its origin. No changes needed.

message.content: are the gizmo visible in the final release or do I have to turn off the show debug area on each of the weapons?

content.text: Gizmos are editor-only â€” they are completely stripped from builds. `OnDrawGizmos` never runs in a standalone build, so you don't need to turn off `showDebugArea` on anything before releasing. The booleans will still exist as serialized fields but they'll have no effect.

import json, sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
text = data[0]['text']

go = obj['gameObject']

# Print top-level RectTransform
for comp in go['components']:
    if comp['type'] == 'RectTransform':
        p = comp['properties']
        print(f\"=== {go['name']} ===\")
        print(f\"  anchorMin: {p['anchorMin']}\")
        print(f\"  anchorMax: {p['anchorMax']}\")
        print(f\"  anchoredPosition: {p['anchoredPosition']}\")
        print(f\"  sizeDelta: {p['sizeDelta']}\")
        print(f\"  pivot: {p['pivot']}\")
        break

# Print children names and RectTransforms
if 'children' in go:

                print(f\"    anchorMin: {p['anchorMin']}\")
                print(f\"    anchorMax: {p['anchorMax']}\")
                print(f\"    anchoredPosition: {p['anchoredPosition']}\")
                print(f\"    sizeDelta: {p['sizeDelta']}\")
                print(f\"    pivot: {p['pivot']}\")
                break

content.text: Also need to update the serialized value on the LobbyManager component in the Lobby scene, since the old "TestMultiplayer" value is baked into the scene.
Successfully loaded scene at path 'Assets/Scenes/Lobby.unity' (additive=false)
Successfully updated component 'LobbyManager' on GameObject 'LobbyManager'
Successfully saved scene 'Lobby' to 'Assets/Scenes/Lobby.unity'

Here's what was fixed:

1. **Wrong scene name** â€” `gameSceneName` was set to "TestMultiplayer" which doesn't exist. Changed to "TestMap" both in the script default and the serialized scene value.

2. **TestMap added to Build Settings** â€” the scene is now registered so `SceneManager.LoadScene` can find it.

3. **Clearer UI feedback** â€” the start button text now shows:
   - "WAITING FOR PLAYERS..." (gray) when < 2 players
   - "WAITING FOR READY..." (white) when players aren't all ready
   - "P1 - PRESS [ATTACK] TO START!" (green) when everyone is ready

The start mechanism: P1 presses the attack button (`D` on keyboard / `B` on gamepad) to ready up, then presses it again when everyone is ready to start the match.

1â†’# Plan: MCP Playtest Server for Unity
     2â†’
     3â†’## Task Description
     4â†’Design and implement a custom MCP (Model Context Protocol) server called **mcp-playtest** that enables an AI agent (Claude Code) to control, observe, and evaluate a Unity game during Play Mode. The MCP integrates into the existing mcp-unity architecture pattern (two-tier: C# Unity Editor side + Node.js TypeScript MCP server) but specifically targets **Play Mode gameplay** â€” something the existing mcp-unity server cannot do since it shuts down during Play Mode transitions.
     5â†’
     6â†’The server provides tools to:
     7â†’1. **Control Play Mode** â€” Enter, exit, pause, unpause, step frame-by-frame
     8â†’2. **Simulate Player Input** â€” Inject movement, attacks, and other game inputs via Unity's Input System
     9â†’3. **Capture Screenshots** â€” Take Game View screenshots and return them as base64 images for AI visual analysis
    10â†’4. **Record Video** â€” Record gameplay clips for later review
    11â†’5. **Query Game State** â€” Read player positions, states, scores, round info, and other runtime data
    12â†’6. **Read Console Logs** â€” Monitor errors/warnings during gameplay
    13â†’
    14â†’## Objective
    15â†’When complete, Claude Code will be able to:
    16â†’- Start a play session in the Unity Editor
    17â†’- Control one or more player characters through simulated input
    18â†’- Capture screenshots at any moment to visually verify behavior
    19â†’- Record short gameplay clips
    20â†’- Query the game state programmatically (player positions, scores, alive count, current state machine state)
    21â†’- Identify bugs, visual issues, and gameplay problems through a combination of visual and data inspection
    22â†’
    23â†’## Problem Statement
    24â†’The existing `mcp-unity` package (CoderGamester) provides excellent Editor-time scene manipulation tools but **explicitly stops its WebSocket server when entering Play Mode** (see `OnPlayModeStateChanged` in `McpUnityServer.cs`). This means no MCP tool can communicate with Unity while the game is running. For a game like BomberSquad â€” a top-down action game with real-time combat â€” the ability to test gameplay at runtime is essential for AI-assisted development.
    25â†’
    26â†’Additionally, there is no existing MCP that combines all of: Play Mode control, input simulation via Unity's new Input System, screenshot capture, and game state introspection in a single package.
    27â†’
    28â†’## Solution Approach
    29â†’
    30â†’### Two-Tier Architecture (matching mcp-unity pattern)
    31â†’
    32â†’```
    33â†’Claude Code â‡„ (stdio) â‡„ Node.js MCP Server â‡„ (WebSocket) â‡„ Unity Editor (C# PlaytestBridge)
    34â†’```
    35â†’
    36â†’**Key difference from mcp-unity**: The Unity-side WebSocket server is designed to **survive Play Mode transitions**. Instead of stopping on `ExitingEditMode`, it uses a persistent `EditorWindow` approach combined with `[ExecuteAlways]` runtime hooks. The WebSocket server runs in editor code that does NOT undergo domain reload destruction (using `SessionState` to persist port/state across reloads).
    37â†’
    38â†’### Play Mode Persistence Strategy
    39â†’
    40â†’The critical challenge is Unity's domain reload during Play Mode entry. Our approach:
    41â†’
    42â†’1. **Editor-side WebSocket server** uses `[InitializeOnLoad]` and persists through Play Mode by:
    43â†’   - NOT stopping on `PlayModeStateChange.ExitingEditMode` (unlike mcp-unity)
    44â†’   - Re-establishing the server on `PlayModeStateChange.EnteredPlayMode` after domain reload
    45â†’   - Using `SessionState` to store server configuration across domain reloads
    46â†’   - Setting `[assembly: UnityEditor.Callbacks.DidReloadScripts]` to auto-restart
    47â†’
    48â†’2. **Runtime data bridge**: An `EditorApplication.update` callback polls runtime game state even from editor code (since editor code can access runtime objects during Play Mode via `FindObjectOfType`, etc.)
    49â†’
    50â†’3. **Input injection**: Uses `InputSystem.QueueStateEvent()` and `InputSystem.QueueDeltaStateEvent()` to inject synthetic input device events. This works at the Input System level, so the game's PlayerInput component receives them naturally.
    51â†’
    52â†’### Screenshot Capture Strategy
    53â†’
    54â†’- **During Play Mode**: Use `ScreenCapture.CaptureScreenshotAsTexture()` called from editor code, encode to PNG, return as base64
    55â†’- **Alternative**: Access the Game View `RenderTexture` via `EditorWindow` reflection and read pixels from it
    56â†’- Images are returned as base64-encoded PNG in the MCP response for Claude's vision analysis
    57â†’
    58â†’### Video Recording Strategy
    59â†’
    60â†’- Use Unity's built-in `UnityEditor.Recorder` package (if available) or a custom frame-capture approach
    61â†’- Custom approach: capture frames at configurable FPS into a temp directory, then use ffmpeg (if available) or return as frame sequence
    62â†’- Simpler MVP: capture a burst of screenshots (e.g., 10 frames over 2 seconds) and return them as an image sequence
    63â†’
    64â†’## Relevant Files
    65â†’
    66â†’### Existing Files (Reference/Integration Points)
    67â†’
    68â†’- `Library/PackageCache/com.gamelovers.mcp-unity@*/Editor/Tools/McpToolBase.cs` â€” Base class pattern for Unity-side tool implementation
    69â†’- `Library/PackageCache/com.gamelovers.mcp-unity@*/Editor/UnityBridge/McpUnityServer.cs` â€” Reference for WebSocket server lifecycle management
    70â†’- `Library/PackageCache/com.gamelovers.mcp-unity@*/Editor/UnityBridge/McpUnitySocketHandler.cs` â€” Reference for WebSocket message routing
    71â†’- `Library/PackageCache/com.gamelovers.mcp-unity@*/Server~/src/index.ts` â€” Reference for Node.js MCP server registration pattern
    72â†’- `Library/PackageCache/com.gamelovers.mcp-unity@*/Server~/src/tools/selectGameObjectTool.ts` â€” Reference for TypeScript tool implementation pattern
    73â†’- `Library/PackageCache/com.gamelovers.mcp-unity@*/Server~/src/unity/mcpUnity.ts` â€” Reference for WebSocket client and message handling
    74â†’- `Assets/Scripts/Character/Player/PlayerController.cs` â€” Player controller to understand input simulation targets
    75â†’- `Assets/Scripts/Game/GameManager.cs` â€” Game state to expose via query tools

   107â†’## Implementation Phases
   108â†’
   109â†’### Phase 1: Foundation
   110â†’1. Set up the Unity-side WebSocket server (`PlaytestServer.cs`) that survives Play Mode
   111â†’2. Set up the Node.js MCP server skeleton with WebSocket client
   112â†’3. Implement the message routing protocol (matching mcp-unity's JSON-RPC 2.0 pattern)
   113â†’4. Implement Play Mode control tool (enter/exit/pause) as the first tool to validate the architecture
   114â†’5. Register in `.mcp.json` and verify end-to-end communication
   115â†’
   116â†’### Phase 2: Core Implementation
   117â†’1. **Screenshot Capture** â€” Implement `CaptureScreenshotTool` with base64 PNG output and Game View render texture capture
   118â†’2. **Input Simulation** â€” Implement `SimulateInputTool` using `InputSystem.QueueStateEvent()` for each action in the Controls.inputactions map
   119â†’3. **Game State Query** â€” Implement `QueryGameStateTool` to read PlayerController states, GameManager state, scores, positions

   126â†’4. **Configuration** â€” Port settings, screenshot resolution, recording FPS via settings file
   127â†’5. **Testing** â€” Manual end-to-end testing of all tools with BomberSquad

   163â†’  - Agent Type: validator

   166â†’## Step by Step Tasks
   167â†’
   168â†’- IMPORTANT: Execute every step in order, top to bottom. Each task maps directly to a `TaskCreate` call.
   169â†’- Before you start, run `TaskCreate` to create the initial task list that all team members can see and execute.
   170â†’
   171â†’### 1. Create Unity-Side WebSocket Server Foundation
   172â†’- **Task ID**: unity-server-foundation
   173â†’- **Depends On**: none
   174â†’- **Assigned To**: builder-unity-server
   175â†’- **Agent Type**: general-purpose
   176â†’- **Parallel**: true (can run alongside task 2)
   177â†’- Create `Assets/Editor/McpPlaytest/` directory structure
   178â†’- Implement `PlaytestServer.cs` â€” singleton WebSocket server using WebSocketSharp (already available from mcp-unity dependency), listens on port 8091 (to avoid conflict with mcp-unity on 8090)
   179â†’- Must persist through Play Mode: on `ExitingEditMode`, do NOT stop; on `EnteredPlayMode`, re-establish server after domain reload using `[DidReloadScripts]` and `SessionState` to remember that server was running
   180â†’- Implement `PlaytestSocketHandler.cs` â€” WebSocket message handler using same JSON-RPC 2.0 protocol as mcp-unity
   181â†’- Implement `PlaytestToolBase.cs` â€” abstract base class for tools (same pattern as `McpToolBase`)
   182â†’- Handle graceful shutdown on editor quit and assembly reload
   183â†’
   184â†’### 2. Create Node.js MCP Server Foundation
   185â†’- **Task ID**: node-server-foundation
   186â†’- **Depends On**: none
   187â†’- **Assigned To**: builder-node-server
   188â†’- **Agent Type**: general-purpose
   189â†’- **Parallel**: true (can run alongside task 1)
   190â†’- Create `Assets/Editor/McpPlaytest/Server~/` directory with package.json (dependencies: `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`, `zod`, `ws`, `uuid`)
   191â†’- Create `tsconfig.json` matching mcp-unity's config (ESM, ES2022 target)
   192â†’- Implement `src/index.ts` â€” MCP server entry point with stdio transport
   193â†’- Implement `src/unity/playtestUnity.ts` â€” WebSocket client connecting to `ws://localhost:8091/McpPlaytest` (copy and simplify from mcp-unity's mcpUnity.ts, no command queue needed for MVP)
   194â†’- Implement `src/utils/logger.ts` â€” simple logger (copy from mcp-unity pattern)
   195â†’- Verify `npm install && npm run build` succeeds
   196â†’
   197â†’### 3. Implement Play Mode Control Tool
   198â†’- **Task ID**: play-mode-control
   199â†’- **Depends On**: unity-server-foundation, node-server-foundation
   200â†’- **Assigned To**: builder-unity-server
   201â†’- **Agent Type**: general-purpose
   202â†’- **Parallel**: false
   203â†’- **Unity side** (`Tools/PlayModeControlTool.cs`):
   204â†’  - `play_mode_control` tool with actions: `enter`, `exit`, `pause`, `unpause`, `step`
   205â†’  - Uses `EditorApplication.isPlaying`, `EditorApplication.isPaused`, `EditorApplication.Step()`
   206â†’  - Returns current play state after action
   207â†’- **Node side** (`tools/playModeControlTool.ts`):
   208â†’  - Register `play_mode_control` tool with zod schema for `action` param (enum of actions)
   209â†’  - Forward to Unity via WebSocket
   210â†’- Register tool in both `PlaytestServer.cs` and `src/index.ts`
   211â†’- Update `.mcp.json` to add the new server entry
   212â†’
   213â†’### 4. Implement Screenshot Capture Tool
   214â†’- **Task ID**: screenshot-capture
   215â†’- **Depends On**: play-mode-control
   216â†’- **Assigned To**: builder-unity-server
   217â†’- **Agent Type**: general-purpose
   218â†’- **Parallel**: true (can run alongside task 5)
   219â†’- **Unity side** (`Tools/CaptureScreenshotTool.cs` + `Utils/ScreenCaptureHelper.cs`):
   220â†’  - `capture_screenshot` tool
   221â†’  - Uses reflection to get the Game View window's `RenderTexture` during Play Mode
   222â†’  - Falls back to `ScreenCapture.CaptureScreenshotAsTexture()` if Game View approach fails
   223â†’  - Encodes texture to PNG, converts to base64 string
   224â†’  - Parameters: `width` (optional, default 1280), `height` (optional, default 720), `superSize` (optional, default 1)
   225â†’  - Returns: `{ success: true, image: "<base64>", width: N, height: N, format: "png" }`
   226â†’  - IMPORTANT: Must execute on main thread using `EditorApplication.delayCall` or async pattern since texture operations require main thread
   227â†’- **Node side** (`tools/captureScreenshotTool.ts`):
   228â†’  - Register `capture_screenshot` tool
   229â†’  - Return the base64 image as an MCP image content block (`type: "image"` with `data` and `mimeType`)
   230â†’
   231â†’### 5. Implement Input Simulation Tool
   232â†’- **Task ID**: input-simulation
   233â†’- **Depends On**: play-mode-control
   234â†’- **Assigned To**: builder-unity-server
   235â†’- **Agent Type**: general-purpose
   236â†’- **Parallel**: true (can run alongside task 4)
   237â†’- **Unity side** (`Tools/SimulateInputTool.cs` + `Utils/InputSimulator.cs`):
   238â†’  - `simulate_input` tool
   239â†’  - Parameters:
   240â†’    - `playerIndex` (int, default 0) â€” which player to target
   241â†’    - `action` (string) â€” one of: "move", "melee_attack", "ranged_attack", "throw_attack", "special_ability"
   242â†’    - `value` (optional object) â€” for "move": `{ x: float, y: float }` (stick/dpad values -1 to 1); for button actions: `{ pressed: bool }`
   243â†’    - `duration` (optional float, default 0.1) â€” how long to hold the input in seconds
   244â†’  - Implementation: find the target player's `PlayerInput` component by `playerIndex`, then use `InputSystem.QueueStateEvent()` to inject a state event on the appropriate device
   245â†’  - For sustained input (like movement), use a coroutine or `EditorApplication.update` callback to hold the input for `duration` seconds, then release
   246â†’  - Returns: `{ success: true, action: "move", playerIndex: 0, appliedValue: {...} }`
   247â†’- **Node side** (`tools/simulateInputTool.ts`):
   248â†’  - Register `simulate_input` tool with full zod schema
   249â†’  - Forward to Unity
   250â†’
   251â†’### 6. Implement Game State Query Tool
   252â†’- **Task ID**: game-state-query
   253â†’- **Depends On**: play-mode-control
   254â†’- **Assigned To**: builder-node-server
   255â†’- **Agent Type**: general-purpose
   256â†’- **Parallel**: true (can run alongside tasks 4, 5)
   257â†’- **Unity side** (`Tools/QueryGameStateTool.cs`):
   258â†’  - `query_game_state` tool
   259â†’  - Parameters: `query` (string enum): "full", "players", "round", "game_mode"
   260â†’  - Reads from `GameManager.instance` at runtime:
   261â†’    - `full`: returns everything â€” currentState, roundNumber, all player data, alive count
   262â†’    - `players`: for each player â€” index, position (transform.position), current state machine state name, kills, deaths, score, isReady, isAlive
   263â†’    - `round`: currentRoundNumber, currentState, roundHandler timer info
   264â†’    - `game_mode`: active GameMode scriptable object fields
   265â†’  - Returns structured JSON with all requested data
   266â†’- **Node side** (`tools/queryGameStateTool.ts`):
   267â†’  - Register tool, forward to Unity, return JSON text response
   268â†’
   269â†’### 7. Implement Console Logs Tool
   270â†’- **Task ID**: console-logs
   271â†’- **Depends On**: unity-server-foundation
   272â†’- **Assigned To**: builder-unity-server
   273â†’- **Agent Type**: general-purpose
   274â†’- **Parallel**: true (can run alongside tasks 4, 5, 6)
   275â†’- **Unity side** (`Tools/GetConsoleLogsTool.cs`):
   276â†’  - `get_playtest_logs` tool
   277â†’  - Subscribes to `Application.logMessageReceived` when server starts
   278â†’  - Stores recent logs in a ring buffer (max 500 entries)
   279â†’  - Parameters: `count` (int, default 50), `filter` (string enum: "all", "error", "warning", "log")
   280â†’  - Returns array of `{ message, stackTrace, type, timestamp }`
   281â†’- **Node side** (`tools/getConsoleLogsTool.ts`):
   282â†’  - Register tool, forward to Unity
   283â†’
   284â†’### 8. Implement Video Recording Tool
   285â†’- **Task ID**: video-recording
   286â†’- **Depends On**: screenshot-capture
   287â†’- **Assigned To**: builder-unity-server
   288â†’- **Agent Type**: general-purpose
   289â†’- **Parallel**: false
   290â†’- **Unity side** (`Tools/RecordVideoTool.cs`):
   291â†’  - `record_video` tool with actions: `start`, `stop`, `status`
   292â†’  - `start`: begins capturing frames at configurable FPS (default 5) using the same ScreenCaptureHelper
   293â†’  - Parameters: `action` (enum), `fps` (int, default 5), `maxDuration` (float, default 5.0 seconds), `width` (int, default 640)
   294â†’  - Captures frames into temp directory as PNG sequence
   295â†’  - `stop`: stops recording, returns array of base64 frame images (or first N if too many)
   296â†’  - `status`: returns isRecording, frameCount, elapsed time
   297â†’  - Cap at 30 frames max to avoid memory issues
   298â†’- **Node side** (`tools/recordVideoTool.ts`):
   299â†’  - Register tool, return frames as array of image content blocks
   300â†’
   301â†’### 9. Wire Up Integration and Configuration
   302â†’- **Task ID**: integration-wiring
   303â†’- **Depends On**: play-mode-control, screenshot-capture, input-simulation, game-state-query, console-logs, video-recording
   304â†’- **Assigned To**: builder-integration
   305â†’- **Agent Type**: general-purpose
   306â†’- **Parallel**: false
   307â†’- Update `.mcp.json` to register the new `mcp-playtest` server pointing to `Assets/Editor/McpPlaytest/Server~/build/index.js`
   308â†’- Ensure all tools are registered in both `PlaytestServer.cs` (RegisterTools) and `src/index.ts`
   309â†’- Run `npm install && npm run build` in the Server~ directory
   310â†’- Verify the MCP server starts and connects to Unity
   311â†’- Create a `PlaytestSettings.json` in ProjectSettings for port configuration
   312â†’- Add the Server~ directory to `.gitignore` for `node_modules/` and `build/`
   313â†’
   314â†’### 10. Final Validation
   315â†’- **Task ID**: validate-all
   316â†’- **Depends On**: integration-wiring
   317â†’- **Assigned To**: validator
   318â†’- **Agent Type**: validator
   319â†’- **Parallel**: false
   320â†’- Verify all C# files compile (no syntax errors, proper using statements)
   321â†’- Verify all TypeScript files compile (`npm run build` succeeds)
   322â†’- Verify `.mcp.json` has correct path for the new server
   323â†’- Verify tool name consistency between C# and TypeScript sides (exact `lower_snake_case` match)
   324â†’- Verify all tools follow the project's code conventions (`this.` prefix, PascalCase/camelCase, no namespaces in game code â€” though editor code can use namespace `McpPlaytest`)
   325â†’- Verify PlaytestServer does NOT conflict with mcp-unity (different port, different WebSocket path)
   326â†’- Verify no files created in Library/, Temp/, Logs/, or obj/ directories
   327â†’
   328â†’## Acceptance Criteria
   329â†’
   330â†’1. **Play Mode Control**: Can enter, exit, pause, and unpause Play Mode via MCP tool calls
   331â†’2. **Screenshot Capture**: Returns a base64-encoded PNG image of the Game View during Play Mode that Claude can analyze visually
   332â†’3. **Input Simulation**: Can move a player character and trigger attacks via MCP tool calls; the game responds to the simulated input as if a real controller was used
   333â†’4. **Game State Query**: Returns structured JSON with player positions, states, scores, and round information during gameplay
   334â†’5. **Console Logs**: Returns recent Unity console log entries including errors and warnings
   335â†’6. **Video Recording**: Can capture a sequence of frames during gameplay and return them for review
   336â†’7. **Persistence**: The MCP server reconnects after Play Mode domain reload without manual intervention
   337â†’8. **No Conflicts**: Runs alongside the existing mcp-unity server without port or functionality conflicts
   338â†’9. **Code Conventions**: All C# code follows BomberSquad conventions; TypeScript follows mcp-unity patterns
   339â†’
   340â†’## Validation Commands
   341â†’Execute these commands to validate the task is complete:
   342â†’
   343â†’- `cd Assets/Editor/McpPlaytest/Server~ && npm install && npm run build` â€” Verify TypeScript compiles
   344â†’- `node Assets/Editor/McpPlaytest/Server~/build/index.js --help 2>&1 || echo "Server starts (stdio mode, will hang waiting for input â€” that's expected)"` â€” Verify server binary runs
   345â†’- `cat .mcp.json | grep mcp-playtest` â€” Verify MCP config includes new server
   346â†’- Check Unity Editor console for "PlaytestServer started on port 8091" after opening the project
   347â†’- In Claude Code, verify the new tools appear in available tools list (capture_screenshot, simulate_input, etc.)
   348â†’
   349â†’## Notes
   350â†’
   351â†’- **WebSocketSharp dependency**: The existing mcp-unity package already includes WebSocketSharp as an editor dependency. Our Editor code in `Assets/Editor/McpPlaytest/` can reference it since it's available in the editor assembly. If it's not accessible, we may need to add a local copy or use a different WebSocket library.
   352â†’- **Domain Reload**: The biggest technical risk is surviving Unity's domain reload when entering Play Mode. The `SessionState` + `[DidReloadScripts]` pattern is well-established for this. If issues arise, an alternative is using `EditorPrefs` to store state.
   353â†’- **Input System Version**: BomberSquad uses Input System 1.18.0. The `InputSystem.QueueStateEvent()` API has been stable since 1.0. We should verify the exact API surface for injecting events on specific devices.
   354â†’- **Screenshot Resolution**: Game View resolution depends on the editor layout. We may need to force a specific resolution via `GameViewUtils` reflection for consistent captures.
   355â†’- **No new packages needed on the Unity side** â€” we leverage WebSocketSharp from mcp-unity and Unity's built-in APIs. The Node.js side needs: `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`, `zod`, `ws` (or `websocket`), `uuid`.
   356â†’- **Namespace**: Unlike the game code (no namespaces), the Editor MCP code should use `namespace McpPlaytest` to avoid collisions with mcp-unity's `McpUnity` namespace.
   357â†’

message.content: I also want to know, would that tool be considered universal for any unity project?

content.text:
Not as-is. It's tightly coupled to this project because it references `GameManager`, `GameState`, and `PlayerController` directly. A universal version would need to:

1. **Find `PlayerInputManager` generically** â€” use `FindObjectsByType<PlayerInputManager>()` instead of going through `GameManager`
2. **Drop the Lobby state check** â€” or make it optional, since not every game has a lobby concept
3. **Just spawn and report** â€” let the game's own systems handle registration

Here's what the universal version would look like:

lines: -            this.Description = "Spawn a new player with a virtual gamepad device. Works in Play Mode when GameManager is in Lobby state. The spawned player can then be controlled via simulate_input.";
+            this.Description = "Spawn a new player with a virtual gamepad device via PlayerInputManager. The spawned player can then be controlled via simulate_input.";
-                return PlaytestSocketHandler.CreateErrorResponse("Cannot spawn: game is not in Lobby state", "invalid_state");
+                return PlaytestSocketHandler.CreateErrorResponse("PlayerInputManager has no player prefab assigned", "invalid_state");
// Create a virtual gamepad for the new player

content.text: Now it's universal â€” it finds `PlayerInputManager` via `FindAnyObjectByType` instead of going through `GameManager`, and has no game-state checks. Any Unity project using `PlayerInputManager` with a player prefab will work with it.

message.content: are there any down sides of using that method?

content.text:
The main downside is it bypasses `PlayerInputManager`'s normal join flow. Specifically:

1. **`OnPlayerJoined` callback may not fire** â€” `PlayerInput.Instantiate` creates the player directly, so `PlayerInputManager`'s `onPlayerJoined` event might not trigger. Any game logic hooked to that event would be skipped. In our case, `GameManager.PollForNewPlayers` polls `PlayerInput.all` every frame instead of relying on that callback, so it works â€” but other projects may not.

2. **Join rules are ignored** â€” `PlayerInputManager` has settings like `maxPlayerCount`, `joinBehavior`, and `enableJoining`. Calling `PlayerInput.Instantiate` bypasses all of those checks.

3. **Virtual gamepad accumulation** â€” each spawn adds a virtual `Gamepad` device to the Input System that persists for the session. If you spawn many players across multiple play sessions without cleanup, you could accumulate stale devices.

The safer alternative would be `PlayerInputManager.JoinPlayer()` which respects the manager's rules and fires callbacks properly. Let me check if that's viable.

-            // Use PlayerInput.Instantiate with the virtual device
+            // Use JoinPlayer which respects PlayerInputManager rules and fires onPlayerJoined

content.text:
Now it uses `PlayerInputManager.JoinPlayer()` instead of `PlayerInput.Instantiate()`. This:

- Fires `onPlayerJoined` callbacks properly
- Respects `maxPlayerCount` and `joiningEnabled` settings
- Goes through the same code path as a real device join

The only remaining caveat is the virtual gamepad accumulation, but those get cleaned up when exiting Play Mode since the Input System resets.

content.text: You're right, apologies. The version using `PlayerInputManager.JoinPlayer()` is already the better universal approach â€” it respects the manager's rules, fires callbacks, and the virtual gamepads clean up on Play Mode exit. No further changes needed, it's good as-is.

All game-specific type references (`GameManager`, `PlayerController`, etc.) only appear in:
- `BomberSquadPlaytestBridge.cs` -- the project-specific adapter (intentional)
- `IPlaytestBridge.cs` -- only as part of interface/method names like `IPlaytestGameState`, `QueryGameMode` (not actual game types)
- `QueryGameStateTool.cs` -- only as part of interface names like `IPlaytestGameState` (not actual game types)
- `PlaytestServer.cs` -- only as `QueryGameStateTool` class name (not modified, as instructed)
- `Server~/src/` TypeScript files -- not modified, as instructed

The universal C# files (`QueryGameStateTool.cs`, `InputSimulator.cs`) have zero references to any BomberSquad-specific types. The refactoring is complete.

## Task Complete

**Task**: Refactor MCP Playtest server C# files to be universal (project-agnostic) by introducing bridge interfaces

**Status**: Completed

**What was done**:
- Created `IPlaytestBridge.cs` with `IPlaytestGameState` and `IPlaytestInputReceiver` interfaces
- Refactored `QueryGameStateTool.cs` to discover `IPlaytestGameState` at runtime via `FindObjectsByType<MonoBehaviour>()`, returning a helpful error if no implementation is found
- Refactored `InputSimulator.cs` to remove all `PlayerController`/`GameManager` references; uses `IPlaytestInputReceiver` for player discovery and button presses, falls back to `PlayerInput.all` and generic gamepad simulation when no bridge is present
- Created `BomberSquadPlaytestBridge.cs` containing all the BomberSquad-specific logic (GameManager access, PlayerData iteration, PlayerController reflection, state machine checks)
- Cleaned up dead code: removed unused `_activeSimulations` dictionary, `InputSimulationState` class, `CheckSimulationExpiry` method, and unused `pascalCase` variable

**Files changed**:
- `D:\Projects\BomberSquad\Assets\Editor\McpPlaytest\IPlaytestBridge.cs` -- new file with `IPlaytestGameState` and `IPlaytestInputReceiver` interfaces
- `D:\Projects\BomberSquad\Assets\Editor\McpPlaytest\Tools\QueryGameStateTool.cs` -- stripped to a thin pass-through that delegates to the interface
- `D:\Projects\BomberSquad\Assets\Editor\McpPlaytest\Utils\InputSimulator.cs` -- removed all project-specific types, added bridge lookup with universal `PlayerInput.all` fallback and action-control-based fallbacks
- `D:\Projects\BomberSquad\Assets\Editor\McpPlaytest\BomberSquadPlaytestBridge.cs` -- new file implementing both interfaces with all the original BomberSquad-specific logic

**Verification**: Grep confirmed zero references to `PlayerController`, `GameManager`, `PlayerData`, `GameState`, `GameMode`, or `RoundHandler` in the universal files. The public API of `InputSimulator.SimulateAction` is unchanged so `SimulateInputTool.cs` requires no modification. No TypeScript files were touched.

The matches in QueryGameStateTool.cs and IPlaytestBridge.cs are just interface names (`IPlaytestGameState`, `QueryGameMode`) â€” not BomberSquad types. InputSimulator.cs has zero project-specific references.

Here's the final architecture:

**Universal files** (work with any Unity 6 project):
- `IPlaytestBridge.cs` â€” defines `IPlaytestGameState` and `IPlaytestInputReceiver` interfaces
- `InputSimulator.cs` â€” uses interfaces with fallback to generic `PlayerInput.all` + gamepad simulation
- `QueryGameStateTool.cs` â€” delegates to `IPlaytestGameState`, returns helpful error if none found
- All other tools (screenshot, video, logs, play mode, spawn player) â€” already universal

**Project-specific adapter** (only file to customize per project):
- `BomberSquadPlaytestBridge.cs` â€” implements both interfaces using `GameManager`, `PlayerController`, etc.

Any Unity 6 project can use the MCP Playtest server by just implementing the two interfaces on a MonoBehaviour in their scene. Without any implementation, the basic tools (screenshots, logs, play mode control, player spawning, generic gamepad input) still work.

content.text:
The current code has three capture strategies in order:

1. **Game View render texture** â€” may fail or return stale content if the window isn't visible
2. **Camera.Render** â€” renders directly to a RenderTexture, works independently of window focus, but only captures `Camera.main` and misses Screen Space Overlay UI
3. **ScreenCapture** â€” requires the window to be focused and visible

I'll restructure this to prioritize the camera-based approach, render ALL cameras (not just main), and remove the unreliable `ScreenCapture` fallback.

content.text:
Compiles clean. Here's what changed:

**Primary method: `CaptureViaAllCameras`** â€” Renders every enabled camera in depth order to a RenderTexture. This works completely independently of window focus since `Camera.Render()` is a programmatic render call. It captures all cameras, not just `Camera.main`, so overlay cameras and post-processing cameras are included.

**Fallback: `CaptureViaGameView`** â€” Reads the Game View's internal render texture with a forced `Repaint()` call. This can capture Screen Space Overlay UI that cameras alone miss, but may not always have up-to-date content when the window is fully obscured.

**Removed: `ScreenCapture.CaptureScreenshotAsTexture()`** â€” This requires the Unity window to be visible and focused, which is exactly what you don't want.

One caveat: Screen Space Overlay canvases (like the lobby UI) are drawn by Unity's Canvas system outside of any camera, so the camera-based approach won't capture them. If you need UI in screenshots, the Game View fallback handles that â€” or you could switch your canvases to Screen Space - Camera mode. For gameplay screenshots without UI, the camera approach works perfectly regardless of focus.
